reasoning: The Lean statement correctly formalizes the informal claim. The type `Set (Set (Set.Icc 1 n))` represents a collection of subsets of {1,...,n}. The condition `∀ S ∈ subsets, ∀ T ∈ subsets, (S ∩ T).Nonempty` is the standard intersecting family condition (any two sets, possibly equal, share a common element), which is the conventional combinatorial reading. The conclusion `∃ m, m ≤ 2^(n-1) ∧ Nonempty (Fin m ≃ subsets)` is logically equivalent to saying |subsets| ≤ 2^(n-1), since the bijection with `Fin m` captures finiteness and cardinality m, and the collection is automatically finite (being a subcollection of the finite powerset of {1,...,n}). The hypothesis `n ≥ 1` is a reasonable implicit premise given the notation "{1, 2, ..., n}" — without it, ℕ-truncated subtraction makes 2^(n-1) behave oddly, though the theorem still holds trivially when n = 0.
